take all reasonable steps to ensure that each dependent child under the age of 5 years is up to date with core checks under—
the programme that immediately before 15 July 2013 was known as Well Child; or
any similar programme established in its place:

ensure that each dependent child under the age of 5 years attends an approved early childhood education programme or other suitable childcare while the young person is in education, training, work-based learning, or part-time work.

If P meets the criteria prescribed in regulations made for the purposes of section 62 under section 418(1)﻿(d), P is entitled to receive the appropriate incentive payment (if any) stated in subpart 3 of Part 6 of Schedule 4 as if he or she were receiving a young parent payment.
P is not subject to the work test, or to work-preparation obligations.
If P is aged under 20 years when the obligations in subsections (2) and (3) start to apply to P, those obligations continue—
until the close of the day that is 6 months after the date on which the obligations started to apply to P, even if he or she turns 20 during that 6-month period; or
if P, on the day on which he or she turns 20, is continuing in a course of education, training, or work-based learning, until,—
if the course is a course of secondary instruction or if the course ends in December, the close of the following 31 March; or
in any other case, the close of the day on which the course ends.
If paragraphs (a) and (b) of subsection (6) both apply to P, those obligations continue until the later of the 2 dates that apply under those paragraphs.

While P is subject to obligations under subsection (2) or (3) (being obligations to which P became subject on or after 25 October 2016), sections 341 and 342 and the obligation in section 162(1)﻿(i) apply to P as if he or she were receiving a youth support payment.

prov.heading: Young person aged 18 or 19 years, who is receiving jobseeker support as spouse or partner of person granted that benefit, or who is work-tested spouse or partner of specified beneficiary, and who is at significant risk of long-term welfare dependency

para.text: This section applies to a young person (P) aged 18 or 19 years without dependent children who—
is receiving jobseeker support under sections 20 to 24 as the spouse or partner of the person granted the benefit; or
is the work-tested spouse or partner of a specified beneficiary (other than a spouse or partner described in paragraph (a)).

If MSD determines that P is at significant risk of long-term welfare dependency (as that risk is defined in subsection (8)), MSD may require P to receive services of a kind referred to in section 373(1)﻿(a) (youth services).

In exercising the discretion under subsection (2), MSD may consider factors that affect MSD’s ability to provide the young person with youth services (for example, funding and capacity to provide the services in the young person’s area).
However, before requiring the young person to receive youth services, MSD must take reasonable steps to consult the young person about—
the young person’s risk of long-term welfare dependency (as that risk is defined in subsection (8)); and
the proposal to require the young person to receive youth services.
A requirement under subsection (2) that is placed on a young person who is aged 19 years at the time the requirement is made continues—
until the close of the day that is 6 months after the date on which the requirement was placed on the young person, even if the young person turns 20 during that 6-month period; or
in the case of a young person who, on the day on which he or she turns 20, is continuing in a course of education, training, or work-based learning, until,—
if the course is a course of secondary instruction or if the course ends in December, the close of the following 31 March:
in any other case, the close of the day on which the course ends.

The following apply to a young person who is required to receive youth services under subsection (2) as if the person’s jobseeker support, or the portion of the jobseeker support or the specified beneficiary’s benefit payable to the young person under section 337, were a youth payment:
the obligations in section 162(1)﻿(b) to (i) in addition to the young person’s work-test obligations or obligations under sections 124 and 125:
sections 55 and 289 as if the appropriate incentive payments were those set out in clauses 8 and 9 in subpart 3 of Part 6 of Schedule 4:
section 288:
sections 339, 340, 341, and 378:
regulations made under section 418(1)﻿(c), (d), (l), and (m).
MSD may from time to time review a requirement placed on a young person under subsection (2) and may confirm or revoke it.

In this section, risk of long-term welfare dependency, in relation to a person, means the risk, determined using risk factors set by the Minister for the purposes of section 165 and this section by direction under section 7, that the person will, for an indefinite period, not be able to obtain full-time employment and will be likely to remain wholly or largely dependent for the person’s financial support on all or part of a main benefit under this Act.

history.history-note: Section 168(6)﻿(e): amended, on 26 May 2025, by section 61 of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).

prov.heading: Interpretation

para.text: In sections 164 to 167,—
approved early childhood education programme means an early childhood education programme approved by regulations made under section 424
core check means a check of a child's health and well-being that is—
done under, and identified in contract or funding arrangements as an essential part of, the programme in question; and
funded, directly or indirectly, through Vote Health
specified beneficiary means a person who is in a relationship and receives in his or her own right—
an emergency benefit, supported living payment under section 34, or jobseeker support; or
New Zealand superannuation paid at a rate specified in clause 1 of Part 2 of Schedule 1 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001; or
a veteran’s pension at the relationship (partner not receiving superannuation or pension) rate specified in regulations referred to in section 169 of the Veterans’ Support Act 2014.

history.history-note: Section 169 specified beneficiary paragraph (b): replaced, on 9 November 2020, by section 37 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).
Section 169 specified beneficiary paragraph (c): replaced, on 9 November 2020, by section 37 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).

subpart.crosshead: Specific obligations: obligation to work with contracted service providers

prov.heading: Obligation to work with contracted service providers

para.text: This section applies to a person (P) who receives 1 of the following benefits, whether in P’s own right or on behalf of P’s spouse or partner:
jobseeker support:
sole parent support:
a supported living payment:
an emergency benefit.
P must, when required by MSD,—
attend and participate in any interview with a contracted service provider specified by MSD:
attend and participate in any assessment of P undertaken on behalf of MSD by a contracted service provider specified by MSD:

co-operate with a contracted service provider specified by MSD in facilitating the provision of the services that the provider has been contracted to provide in relation to P’s obligations under all or any of the following:
conditions of an emergency benefit that MSD imposes or determines under section 63(5):
work-preparation obligations (see sections 124 and 125):
obligations in relation to dependent children (see sections 131 to 135):
work-test obligations (see sections 144 and 146):
report to a contracted service provider specified by MSD on P's compliance with P's obligations under this Act as often as, and in the manner that, the provider reasonably requires.

prov.heading: Person who fails to comply with requirement under section 170(2) subject to sanction

para.text: A person (P) is subject to a sanction (see section 233(f)) if MSD is satisfied that P has, without a good and sufficient reason, failed to comply with a requirement to take any of the steps listed in section 170(2).

subpart.crosshead: General obligations: obligations in relation to overseas pensions

prov.heading: Interpretation

para.text: In sections 173 to 180,—
NZ benefits legislation means all or any of the following enactments:
this Act:
the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001:

text.citation: Part 6 of the Veterans’ Support Act 2014

para.text: overseas pension has the same meaning given to it by section 187 for the purposes of sections 187 to 190.

prov.heading: Applicant for benefit, etc, to take reasonable steps to obtain overseas pension

para.text: This section applies if—
a person (A) is an applicant for a benefit under the NZ benefits legislation; and
1 or more of the following persons may be entitled to an overseas pension or may be granted an overseas pension:
A:
A’s spouse or partner:
A’s dependant.
If this section applies,—
A and A’s spouse or partner must take all reasonable steps to obtain the overseas pension to which either or both of them may be entitled or that may be granted to either or both of them; and
A must take all reasonable steps to obtain the overseas pension to which A’s dependant may be entitled or that may be granted to A’s dependant.

prov.heading: MSD may give notice requiring person to take reasonable steps to obtain overseas pension

para.text: MSD may give a notice to any of the following persons requiring that person to take all reasonable steps to obtain an overseas pension to which that person may be entitled or which that person may be granted:
an applicant (A) for a benefit under the NZ benefits legislation:
a recipient (R) of a benefit under the NZ benefits legislation:
the spouse or partner of A or R.
The notice must—
be in writing; and
specify that the person to whom the notice is given must take all reasonable steps to obtain the overseas pension for that person; and
specify the period during which the person must take the necessary steps.

prov.heading: MSD may give notice requiring person to take reasonable steps to obtain overseas pension for dependant

para.text: MSD may give a notice to any of the following persons requiring that person to take all reasonable steps to obtain an overseas pension to which his or her dependant may be entitled or which his or her dependant may be granted:
an applicant for a benefit under the NZ benefits legislation:
a recipient of a benefit under any of those enactments.
The notice must—
be in writing; and
specify that the person to whom the notice is given must take all reasonable steps to obtain the overseas pension for his or her dependant; and
specify the period during which the person must take the necessary steps.

prov.heading: Failure to comply with MSD’s notice given under section 174 or 175

para.text: This section applies if a person to whom a notice has been given under section 174 or 175 fails to comply with that notice.
MSD may—
in the case of an applicant for a benefit, refuse to grant the benefit:
in the case of a beneficiary or his or her spouse or partner, suspend the benefit.
A benefit suspended under subsection (2)﻿(b) remains suspended until—
the beneficiary or his or her spouse or partner satisfies MSD that he or she has taken all reasonable steps to obtain the overseas pension; or
MSD cancels the benefit under subsection (4).
MSD may, not less than 40 working days after suspension of a benefit under subsection (2)﻿(b), cancel the benefit from the date that MSD determines.

prov.heading: Applicant for benefit must provide information as to rate of overseas pension

para.text: This section applies if a person (A) applies for a benefit under the NZ benefits legislation.
A must provide MSD with information establishing to MSD’s satisfaction the rate of any overseas pension that is received by any of the following persons:
A:
A’s spouse or partner:
A’s dependant.
MSD may refuse to grant a benefit to an applicant who has not complied with subsection (2) within 10 working days after applying for the benefit.

prov.heading: MSD may give notice requiring beneficiary to provide information as to rate of overseas pension

para.text: This section applies if a person (P) receives a benefit under the NZ benefits legislation.
MSD may give P a written notice requiring P to provide MSD with information establishing to MSD’s satisfaction the rate of any overseas pension that is received by any of the following persons:
P:
P’s spouse or partner:
P’s dependant.

prov.heading: Failure to comply with MSD’s notice given under section 178

para.text: This section applies if a person to whom a notice has been given under section 178 fails to comply with that notice within 10 working days after the notice has been given.
MSD may suspend the benefit, which remains suspended until—
the beneficiary establishes to MSD’s satisfaction the rate of the overseas pension; or
MSD cancels the benefit under subsection (3).
MSD may, not less than 40 working days after suspension of a benefit under subsection (2), cancel the benefit from the date that MSD determines.

prov.heading: Meaning of rate

para.text: In sections 177 to 179, rate, in relation to an overseas pension, means the rate of that pension in the currency of the country paying that pension.

prov.heading: Application of health and safety legislation, etc

para.text: This section applies if a person (P)—
is doing work as part of a job-search activity—
in accordance with P’s work-test obligations; or
under section 236E (sanction for first failure: report job search: general); or
under section 261 (how person recomplies after failure to comply with obligation); or
under section 261C (how person recomplies after failure that resulted in imposition of report job search); or
under section 324 (effect of participation in certain activities on non-entitlement period); or
is a beneficiary and is doing work as part of—
an employment-related activity arranged by MSD; or
an activity in the community arranged by MSD.
The Health and Safety at Work Act 2015 and the Human Rights Act 1993 apply to P, and to the person (B) providing the work that person P is doing, as if P were—
a worker in B’s business or undertaking; or
B’s employee.
Except as provided in subsection (2), nothing in this Part creates or implies an employment relationship between P and B.

history.history-note: Section 181(1)﻿(a): replaced, on 20 October 2025, by section 9(1) of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).
Section 181(1)﻿(b): replaced, on 26 May 2025, by section 9(2) of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).

prov.heading: Obligations suspended where MSD has exercised discretion to pay benefit while beneficiary overseas

para.text: This section applies if MSD has exercised its discretion under any regulations made under section 436 to pay a benefit to a person (P) for a period of absence by P from New Zealand.
During the period of absence for which the benefit is paid, P is not required to comply with his or her work-test or work-preparation obligations or young person or young parent obligations.

part.heading: Factors affecting benefits

prov.heading: What this Part does

para.text: This Part sets out the factors that may affect a benefit.
The factors affecting a benefit are:
jobseeker profiles:
pre-benefit activities:
insurance recovery:
entitlement to an overseas pension:
shared care of a dependent child:
compensation or damages:
the grant of a veteran’s entitlement:
a personal benefit excluding a benefit on behalf of a spouse or partner:
a maintenance claim:
a family protection claim:
not being ordinarily resident in New Zealand:
refugee or protected person status:
hospitalisation:
alcohol and drug treatment:
the issue of a warrant for a beneficiary’s arrest:
custody in prison or on remand:
absence from New Zealand:
voluntary unemployment or loss of employment through misconduct, etc:
a non-entitlement period, stand down, or 100% suspension of benefit.

history.history-note: Section 183(2)﻿(aaa): inserted, on 26 May 2025, by section 10 of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).
Section 183(2)﻿(d): repealed, on 1 April 2020, by section 5 of the Social Assistance Legislation (Budget 2019 Welfare Package) Amendment Act 2019 (2019 No 23).
Section 183(2)﻿(r): repealed, on 8 November 2021, by section 6 of the Social Security (Subsequent Child Policy Removal) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 38).

subpart.heading: Factors affecting benefits

history.history-note: Heading: inserted, on 26 May 2025, by section 11 of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).

prov.label: 183A
prov.heading: Requirement to have completed jobseeker profile

para.text: This section applies if—
MSD is contacted by a person (P) (or by some other person acting on P’s behalf) requesting financial assistance under this Act for P; and
the request for financial assistance for P is not a request for the regrant under section 336 of a specified benefit (as defined in section 331) that is—
jobseeker support; or
sole parent support; and
MSD considers that—
the appropriate financial assistance would be a benefit that is jobseeker support or sole parent support; or

the appropriate financial assistance would be a benefit that is an emergency benefit and, if that financial assistance were granted to P, P would, or would likely, be subject to conditions that are or include that P be available for, and take reasonable steps to obtain, suitable employment.
MSD must require P to have a jobseeker profile completed to MSD’s satisfaction.

prov.body.subprov.crosshead: Requirement for specified spouse or partner of P

para.text: If P has a spouse or partner who is not, in the spouse’s or partner’s own right, receiving or applying for a main benefit under this Act, New Zealand superannuation, or a veteran’s pension, and who is not, or is not likely to be, subject to obligations under section 166 or 167, MSD must also require P’s spouse or partner to have a jobseeker profile completed to MSD’s satisfaction.
In this section and sections 183B to 183D, jobseeker profile means a form provided—
by or on behalf of MSD to P or P’s spouse or partner; and
to enable P or P’s spouse or partner, by completing the form, to record P’s or P’s spouse or partner’s work experience, qualifications, and work preferences.
A requirement under subsection (2) or (3)—
may be oral or written; and
may be revoked orally or in writing if, and only if, MSD considers the requirement is not reasonable in the circumstances of P, of P’s spouse or partner, or of both.

history.history-note: Section 183A: inserted, on 26 May 2025, by section 11 of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).

prov.label: 183B
prov.heading: If person does not have jobseeker profile completed

para.text: MSD is not required under section 298(1) to inquire into a claim for any benefit referred to in section 183A(1) made by or on behalf of a person (P) specified in section 183A(1) unless—
P has the jobseeker profile completed to MSD’s satisfaction; or
MSD has revoked the requirement under section 183A(2) for P to have the jobseeker profile completed to MSD’s satisfaction.
Subsection (1) applies whether or not an application for a benefit has been completed by or on behalf of P.
Any application for a benefit made by or on behalf of P lapses unless—
P has the jobseeker profile completed to MSD’s satisfaction within the period allowed under section 183D(1); or
MSD has revoked the requirement under section 183A(2) for P to have the jobseeker profile completed to MSD’s satisfaction.

history.history-note: Section 183B: inserted, on 26 May 2025, by section 11 of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).

prov.label: 183C
prov.heading: If person’s spouse or partner does not have jobseeker profile completed

para.text: This section applies if—
a person specified in section 183A(1) (P) has a spouse or partner who is required to have a jobseeker profile completed to MSD’s satisfaction under section 183A(3); and
P’s spouse or partner fails to have the jobseeker profile completed to MSD’s satisfaction within the period allowed under section 183D(1).
If this section applies under subsection (1), it applies until—
P’s spouse or partner has the jobseeker profile completed to MSD’s satisfaction; or
MSD revokes the requirement under section 183A(3) for P’s spouse or partner to have the jobseeker profile completed to MSD’s satisfaction.
P receives the following forms of assistance (if all or any of them is granted) at the following rate or rates only:
any benefit referred to in section 183A(1) that P is granted, at half of the rate of that benefit payable to a beneficiary—
who is in a relationship; and
whose spouse or partner is not granted a benefit in the spouse’s or partner’s own right; and

the accommodation supplement, temporary additional support, or both (if any), to which P and P’s spouse or partner would apart from this subsection be entitled, at half of the otherwise applicable rate or rates.

history.history-note: Section 183C: inserted, on 26 May 2025, by section 11 of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).

prov.label: 183D
prov.heading: Period allowed for completion

para.text: The period allowed (see sections 183B(3)﻿(a) and 183C(1)﻿(b)) is—
the period that—
starts on the date of first contact by or on behalf of P; and
ends on the 20th working day after that date; or
a longer period allowed by MSD under subsection (2).

Before or after the end of the 20-working-day period referred to in subsection (1)﻿(a), MSD may allow P or P’s spouse or partner a longer period if MSD considers that good and sufficient reason exists for P or P’s spouse or partner not being able to have the jobseeker profile completed to MSD’s satisfaction within that 20-working-day period.

history.history-note: Section 183D: inserted, on 26 May 2025, by section 11 of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).

prov.heading: Regulations may prescribe pre-benefit activities

para.text: MSD may, in accordance with regulations made under section 432,—
require a person who contacts MSD requesting financial assistance under this Act or that person’s spouse or partner (if any) to undertake 1 or more pre-benefit activities; and
impose consequences for non-compliance, without a good and sufficient reason, with a requirement to undertake the 1 or more pre-benefit activities.

prov.heading: MSD must explain requirements

para.text: MSD must take reasonable and appropriate steps to make every person on whom any requirements are placed under regulations made under section 432 (the regulations) aware of the following:
the person's obligations (as set out in the regulations) in relation to the requirements; and
the consequences (as set out in the regulations) of failure to comply with the requirements; and
when a requirement is to be, or has been, revoked, and when the revocation takes, or took, effect.

prov.heading: Benefit may be subject to repayment of insurance payment

para.text: MSD may, in accordance with regulations made under section 433, make the grant of a benefit or the rate of the benefit subject to repayment to MSD of all or part of an insurance payment of a kind or description, or that falls within a class or definition, prescribed by those regulations.

subpart.crosshead: Factors reducing benefits: entitlement to overseas pensions

prov.heading: Interpretation

para.text: In this section and in sections 188 to 190,—
NZ benefits legislation means all or any of the following enactments:
this Act:
Part 1 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001:

text.citation: Part 6 of the Veterans’ Support Act 2014

para.text: overseas pension means a benefit, pension, or periodical allowance, or any part of it, that—
is granted elsewhere than in New Zealand; and
is determined by MSD to be a payment that is part of a programme that—
provides benefits, pensions, or periodical allowances for any of the contingencies for which benefits, pensions, or allowances may be paid under NZ benefits legislation; and
is administered by or on behalf of the Government of the country from which the benefit, pension, or periodical allowance is received; and
is not a Government occupational pension (as defined in Schedule 2); and
is not determined by MSD to be in the nature of, and paid for similar purposes as,—
compensation for injury or death for which payment could be made under the Accident Compensation Act 2001 if the injury or death had occurred in New Zealand after the commencement of that Act; or

a war pension or allowance granted under the Veterans’ Support Act 2014 of a type that would not affect any recipient’s entitlement to a benefit under sections 18 and 199 to 201 of this Act unless the pension or allowance is a pension or payment granted under Part 6 of the Veterans’ Support Act 2014; or
a disability allowance; and
is not determined by MSD to be attributable to any voluntary contributions made by or on behalf of the person to whom it is granted or the person’s spouse, partner, or parent.

history.history-note: Section 187 overseas pension paragraph (e): inserted, on 9 November 2020, by section 38 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).

prov.heading: Persons affected by receipt of overseas pension

para.text: A person (P) is a person affected by the receipt of an overseas pension if P is a person who is qualified to receive a benefit under the NZ benefits legislation and—
P is entitled to receive or receives an overseas pension in respect of any of the following persons:
P:
P’s spouse or partner:
any dependant of P; or
P’s spouse or partner is entitled to receive or receives an overseas pension; or
any of P’s dependants is entitled to receive or receives an overseas pension.

prov.heading: Benefit of person affected is reduced by amount of overseas pension

para.text: The rate of the benefit or benefits that would otherwise be payable under the NZ benefits legislation to a person affected by the receipt of an overseas pension must be reduced by the amount of the overseas pension as determined by MSD under regulations made under section 434.

The rate of New Zealand superannuation or a veteran’s pension that a person is qualified to receive must not be reduced under this section by any amount of an overseas pension if the New Zealand superannuation or veteran’s pension is payable to the person overseas under section 26 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001 or section 182 of the Veterans’ Support Act 2014.

The rate of New Zealand superannuation or a veteran’s pension, or related winter energy payment, that a person is qualified to receive must not be reduced under this section by any amount of an overseas pension that—
the person is entitled to receive or receives in respect of the person’s spouse or partner (as referred to in section 188(a)﻿(ii)); or
the person’s spouse or partner is entitled to receive or receives (as referred to in section 188(b)).
See clause 82 of Schedule 1 in relation to the application of subsection (3) to persons who are qualified to receive New Zealand superannuation or a veteran’s pension at certain grandparented rates.

The rate of New Zealand superannuation or a veteran’s pension, or related winter energy payment, that a person is qualified to receive must not be reduced under this section by any amount of an overseas pension that—
the person is entitled to receive or receives in respect of any of the person’s dependants (as referred to in section 188(a)﻿(iii)); or
any of the person’s dependants is entitled to receive or receives (as referred to in section 188(c)).

In this section, a person is qualified to receive a related winter energy payment if the person is entitled to receive a winter energy payment under section 72 because New Zealand superannuation or a veteran’s pension is payable to the person.

history.history-note: Section 189(2): replaced, on 9 November 2020, by section 39 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).
Section 189(3): inserted, on 9 November 2020, by section 39 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).
Section 189(4): inserted, on 9 November 2020, by section 39 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).
Section 189(5): inserted, on 9 November 2020, by section 39 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).
Section 189(6): inserted, on 9 November 2020, by section 39 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).

prov.heading: MSD may enter into arrangement with person affected by receipt of overseas pension

para.text: MSD may, in accordance with regulations made under section 434, enter into an arrangement with a person affected by the receipt of an overseas pension (P) to pay P the rate of a benefit or benefits under the NZ benefits legislation in consideration of MSD receiving an amount that is equivalent to the amount of the overseas pension in question.

prov.heading: Treatment of certain overseas benefits, pensions, and allowances that are not overseas pensions

para.text: This section applies to a benefit, pension, or periodical allowance, or any part of it, that—
is granted elsewhere than in New Zealand; and
is administered by or on behalf of the Government of the country from which the benefit, pension, or periodical allowance is received; and
is determined by MSD to be in the nature of, and paid for similar purposes as,—
compensation for injury or death for which payment could be made under the Accident Compensation Act 2001 if the injury or death had occurred in New Zealand after the commencement of that Act; or

a war pension or allowance granted under the Veterans’ Support Act 2014 of a type that would not affect any recipient’s entitlement to a benefit under sections 18 and 199 to 201 of this Act unless the pension or allowance is a pension or payment granted under Part 6 of the Veterans’ Support Act 2014; or
a disability allowance.

MSD must treat a benefit, pension, or periodical allowance, or any part of it, to which this section applies as if it were in fact the relevant compensation, pension, or allowance specified in subsection (1)﻿(c) (see, for example, sections 18, 87, and 199 to 201).

subpart.crosshead: Factors reducing benefits: failure to assist child support

history.history-note: Heading: repealed, on 1 April 2020, by section 6(b) of the Social Assistance Legislation (Budget 2019 Welfare Package) Amendment Act 2019 (2019 No 23).

prov.heading: MSD must reduce rates of benefits for sole parents for failure to assist child support

history.history-note: Section 192: repealed, on 1 April 2020, by section 6(a) of the Social Assistance Legislation (Budget 2019 Welfare Package) Amendment Act 2019 (2019 No 23).

prov.heading: No reduction in certain cases

history.history-note: Section 193: repealed, on 1 April 2020, by section 6(a) of the Social Assistance Legislation (Budget 2019 Welfare Package) Amendment Act 2019 (2019 No 23).

prov.heading: Additional reduction in certain cases

history.history-note: Section 194: repealed, on 1 April 2020, by section 6(a) of the Social Assistance Legislation (Budget 2019 Welfare Package) Amendment Act 2019 (2019 No 23).

subpart.crosshead: Factors affecting eligibility for benefit: shared care of dependent child

prov.heading: Shared care of dependent child

para.text: This section applies if—
the parents of a dependent child live apart; and
both parents receive benefits; and
each parent has the primary responsibility for the care of the child for at least 40% of the time.
In assessing the benefit and rate of benefit of each parent, MSD must treat the child as a dependent child in assessing the benefit and rate of the primarily responsible parent only.
In subsection (2), the primarily responsible parent is—
the parent who MSD decides has the greater responsibility for the child; or
if MSD is unable to make a decision under paragraph (a), the parent who MSD decides was the child’s principal caregiver immediately before the parents began living apart; or
if MSD is unable to make a decision under paragraph (a) or (b), the parent who the parents agree is the primarily responsible parent.

If the primarily responsible parent cannot be identified under subsection (3), MSD must not take the child into account in assessing the benefit and rate of benefit of either parent until the parents agree who is the primarily responsible parent.

prov.heading: Rules for assessing which parent has greater responsibility for dependent child

para.text: For the purposes of deciding under section 195(3)﻿(a) which parent has greater responsibility for the child, MSD must have regard primarily to the time that the child is in the care of each parent and then to the following factors:
how the responsibility for decisions about the daily activities of the child is shared; and
who is responsible for taking the child to and from school and supervising that child's leisure activities; and
how decisions about the education or health care of the child are made; and
the financial arrangements for the child's material support; and
which parent pays for which expenses of the child.

subpart.crosshead: Factors reducing benefits: compensation or damages

prov.heading: Effect of compensation or damages on application for benefit

para.text: This section applies if—
a person (P) applies for a benefit; and
P has been paid or has a claim for compensation or damages from any other person in respect of—
any disability caused by accident or disease; or
wrongful or unjustifiable dismissal.

If P has been paid compensation or damages, MSD may refuse the benefit or grant a reduced benefit for the period of disability or loss of expectation of employment for which the compensation or damages have been paid or deemed by MSD to have been paid.

If P has a claim for compensation or damages, MSD may grant the benefit on the condition that MSD may require P to repay MSD all or part of the benefit out of the compensation or damages that are paid to P as a result of the claim.
If a benefit or part of a benefit is granted on the condition set out in subsection (3), the benefit or the part of it—
constitutes a charge on the compensation or damages; and
is a debt due to the Crown under regulations made under section 444 from the beneficiary or any person liable for the payment of the compensation or damages; and
is subject to recovery under regulations made under section 444 from the beneficiary or any person liable for the payment of the compensation or damages.
In this section, compensation or damages—
includes any ex gratia payment made in settlement or on account of a claim for compensation or damages; but
does not include any impairment lump sum received under Schedule 1 of the Accident Compensation Act 2001.

prov.heading: Loss of earnings compensation under Accident Compensation Act 2001

para.text: The purpose of this section is to require MSD, before a specified benefit payable to a person (P) is paid, to reduce the rate of that specified benefit that would, but for this section, be payable to P, in order to prevent the situation that is the occurrence of both of the following:
P receives, in respect of a period, the rate of the specified benefit that would, but for this section, be payable to P; and
P or P’s spouse or partner receives, in respect of all or part of the same period, amounts of weekly loss of earnings compensation.
This section applies if—
a person (P) is entitled to a specified benefit as defined in subsection (3); and
either of the following persons is entitled to receive or receives weekly loss of earnings compensation:
P, on P’s own account or in respect of P’s spouse or partner or of P’s dependent child:
P’s spouse or partner.

prov.body.subprov.crosshead: Duty to reduce rate of specified benefit by amount of weekly loss of earnings compensation

para.text: MSD must reduce the rate of the specified benefit payable to P by the amount of weekly loss of earnings compensation received by P or P’s spouse or partner (unless this subsection does not apply because of the exception in clause 31 of Schedule 1).

prov.body.subprov.crosshead: Meanings of specified benefit and weekly loss of earnings compensation

para.text: In this section and section 198A,—

specified benefit means a main benefit under this Act, an orphan’s benefit, an unsupported child’s benefit, and a veteran’s pension subject to abatement under section 171 of the Veterans’ Support Act 2014
weekly loss of earnings compensation means weekly compensation for loss of earnings or loss of potential earning capacity payable to a person under the Accident Compensation Act 2001.

prov.body.subprov.crosshead: This section does not limit section 252 of Accident Compensation Act 2001, etc

para.text: This section does not limit—

section 252 of the Accident Compensation Act 2001 (under which a specified benefit, after it is received in respect of a period, is regarded as having been paid in respect of a later-established claim to an entitlement from the Accident Compensation Corporation in respect of all or part of the same period); or
section 198A of this Act (how section 252 of Accident Compensation Act 2001 affects entitlement to, and rate of, specified supplementary assistance).

history.history-note: Section 198(1AAA) heading: inserted, on 11 March 2026, by section 4(1) of the Social Security (Accident Compensation and Calculation of Weekly Income) Amendment Act 2026 (2026 No 6).
Section 198(1AAA): inserted, on 11 March 2026, by section 4(1) of the Social Security (Accident Compensation and Calculation of Weekly Income) Amendment Act 2026 (2026 No 6).
Section 198(1) heading: inserted, on 11 March 2026, by section 4(1) of the Social Security (Accident Compensation and Calculation of Weekly Income) Amendment Act 2026 (2026 No 6).
Section 198(2) heading: inserted, on 11 March 2026, by section 4(2) of the Social Security (Accident Compensation and Calculation of Weekly Income) Amendment Act 2026 (2026 No 6).
Section 198(3) heading: inserted, on 11 March 2026, by section 4(3) of the Social Security (Accident Compensation and Calculation of Weekly Income) Amendment Act 2026 (2026 No 6).
Section 198(3): amended, on 11 March 2026, by section 4(4) of the Social Security (Accident Compensation and Calculation of Weekly Income) Amendment Act 2026 (2026 No 6).
Section 198(4) heading: inserted, on 11 March 2026, by section 4(5) of the Social Security (Accident Compensation and Calculation of Weekly Income) Amendment Act 2026 (2026 No 6).
Section 198(4): inserted, on 11 March 2026, by section 4(5) of the Social Security (Accident Compensation and Calculation of Weekly Income) Amendment Act 2026 (2026 No 6).

prov.label: 198A
prov.heading: How section 252 of Accident Compensation Act 2001 affects entitlement to, and rate of, specified supplementary assistance

para.text: This section applies only if MSD is satisfied that—
a beneficiary has received a payment of an instalment of a specified benefit in respect of a period under section 252(1)﻿(a) of the Accident Compensation Act 2001; and
the beneficiary has later established a claim to an entitlement from the Accident Compensation Corporation in respect of all or part of the same period under section 252(1)﻿(b) of that Act; and
all of the payment of the instalment is an excess benefit payment under section 252(2), (3), and (5) of that Act and is refunded, or is required to be refunded, under section 252(4) of that Act; and
the beneficiary has received 1 or more payments of specified supplementary assistance in respect of the same period under this Act.

prov.body.subprov.crosshead: Review of entitlement to, and rate of, specified supplementary assistance

para.text: MSD must assess the following matters:
whether the beneficiary was entitled to receive the specified supplementary assistance that was paid to them:
whether the beneficiary was entitled to receive the rate of specified supplementary assistance that was paid to them.
MSD must assess those matters—
in a review under section 304 or 310B if MSD is satisfied because of the review that this section applies; or
in any other case, in a review under section 304 that this paragraph requires MSD to carry out if MSD is satisfied that this section applies.
In assessing those matters, MSD (and the appeal authority, or a court, on an appeal against a decision made by MSD) must take the beneficiary—
not to have been entitled to the instalment of the specified benefit; and
not to have received the instalment of the specified benefit.
In this section, specified supplementary assistance means all or any of the following assistance:
accommodation supplement:
winter energy payment:
disability allowance under subpart 14 of Part 2 (whether it is—
disability allowance under section 85; or
special disability allowance under section 89):
temporary additional support:
a special benefit continued under section 23 of the Social Security (Working for Families) Amendment Act 2004 (as that section is saved by clause 19 of Schedule 1 of this Act):
special assistance under the Temporary GST Assistance Programme—
approved and established under section 124(1)﻿(d) of the Social Security Act 1964 on 27 August 2010; and
saved by clause 21 of Schedule 1 of this Act as if it were approved and established under section 101 of this Act.

history.history-note: Section 198A: inserted, on 11 March 2026, by section 5 of the Social Security (Accident Compensation and Calculation of Weekly Income) Amendment Act 2026 (2026 No 6).

prov.heading: Veteran’s entitlement excludes any other benefit

para.text: A person (P) who is granted an entitlement under the Veterans’ Support Act 2014 or in respect of whom an entitlement under that Act is payable is not entitled to any other benefit under this Act.
Subsection (1) is subject to section 200.

prov.heading: Exceptions to rule that veteran’s entitlement excludes any other benefit

para.text: Section 199(1) does not apply if the entitlement under the Veterans’ Support Act 2014 is any of the following:
a pension or allowance granted to P on the ground of P’s disablement:
a pension or allowance under Part 6 of the Veterans’ Support Act 2014:
a surviving spouse or partner pension granted under Part 3 of the Veterans’ Support Act 2014:
an allowance for services rendered as an attendant on a disabled member of the New Zealand or any other Commonwealth forces or of the Emergency Reserve Corps or of the New Zealand mercantile marine.
Section 199(1) does not exclude a person who is paid a children’s pension under section 72 of the Veterans’ Support Act 2014 from eligibility for any of the following benefits:
a supported living payment on the ground of restricted work capacity or total blindness:
jobseeker support on the ground of health condition, injury, or disability:

a youth payment or young parent payment granted to a person who qualifies for an exemption from the obligation to undertake education or training or work-based learning (as defined in section 162(4)) on the ground of the person’s health condition (for example, sickness), injury, or disability:
a child disability allowance.

subpart.crosshead: Factors affecting benefit: personal benefit excludes benefit on behalf

prov.heading: Personal benefit of spouse or partner excludes benefit on behalf of spouse or partner

para.text: This section applies to a part of a benefit if the part is payable in respect of a person’s (P’s) spouse or partner.
The part must not be paid for a period when a personal benefit is payable in respect of P’s spouse or partner.
This section is subject to regulations made under section 442(2)﻿(e) (regulations: payments: apportionment) for the purposes of section 337 (how benefits are paid).

prov.heading: MSD may refuse or cancel benefit for failure to take reasonable steps to obtain maintenance

para.text: This section applies if MSD is satisfied an applicant has failed to take reasonable steps to obtain, or has foregone rights to, maintenance the applicant may be entitled to in respect of the applicant under the Family Proceedings Act 1980 or any other Act.
MSD may refuse to grant a benefit, cancel or reduce a benefit already granted, or grant a benefit at a reduced rate.

subpart.crosshead: Factors affecting benefit: family protection claim

prov.heading: MSD may refuse or cancel benefit if family protection claim not pursued

para.text: This section applies if a person (P) who is a beneficiary or an applicant for a benefit, in MSD’s view,—
has a tenable claim under the Family Protection Act 1955 for a share of or provision out of the estate of a deceased relative; and
has failed to take reasonable steps to advance that claim, including bringing or participating in court proceedings to enforce P’s claim.
MSD may refuse to grant the benefit, grant it at a reduced rate, or cancel a benefit already granted.
MSD may intervene in court proceedings for enforcing P’s claim under the Family Protection Act 1955 and may give evidence or make submissions in support of P’s claim.

subpart.crosshead: Factors affecting benefit: person not ordinarily resident in New Zealand

prov.heading: MSD may refuse or cancel benefit if person not ordinarily resident in New Zealand

para.text: MSD may refuse to grant a benefit, cancel or reduce a benefit already granted, or grant a benefit at a reduced rate, if MSD is satisfied that the applicant, or the spouse or partner of the applicant or any person in respect of whom the benefit or any part of the benefit is or would be payable, is not ordinarily resident in New Zealand.
This section does not apply to—
New Zealand superannuation being paid overseas under section 26 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001 (see section 29(4A) of that Act); or

New Zealand superannuation payable to a person resident in a specified Pacific country under section 31 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001 (see section 35(2) of that Act); or
a veteran’s pension being paid overseas under section 182 of the Veterans’ Support Act 2014 (see section 189(3) of that Act); or
a veteran’s pension payable to a person resident in a specified Pacific country under section 191 of the Veterans’ Support Act 2014 (see section 195(2) of that Act):
a benefit payable under any reciprocity agreement with another country adopted by an order made under section 380.

subpart.crosshead: Factors affecting benefit: refugee or protected person status

prov.heading: Refugee or protected person status

para.text: This section applies if MSD is satisfied that a person (P) is any of the following:
a person lawfully present in New Zealand who is awaiting the outcome of the person’s claim for recognition as a refugee or a protected person:
a person who is recognised as a refugee or protected person:
a person applying for a residence class visa under the Immigration Act 2009 who is compelled to remain in New Zealand because of unforeseen circumstances.
MSD may grant P—
an emergency benefit under section 63:
temporary additional support under section 96.

This section overrides section 16 (residential requirement) and section 19 (general limitation on receiving benefit: persons unlawfully resident or present in New Zealand and persons holding temporary entry class visa).

prov.heading: Hospitalisation

para.text: The main benefit under this Act of a person (P) is affected by hospitalisation if P—
is in hospital for a period of more than 13 weeks; and
has a spouse or partner but no dependent children or is single.
The rate of P’s benefit after the 13th week is the rate specified in Part 12 of Schedule 4 except that MSD may pay P a higher rate after taking into account P’s personal financial circumstances.
The rate of benefit that is paid to P under subsection (2) is not affected by any absence or absences from hospital of P—
after the 13th week of hospitalisation; and
lasting no longer than 28 days.

prov.heading: Benefit of spouse or partner increased after 13th week of hospitalisation

para.text: This section applies if—
a person’s (P’s) main benefit under this Act is reduced under section 206; and
P has a spouse or partner who is not in hospital or has been in hospital for less than 13 weeks; and
a specified benefit is payable to or in respect of P’s spouse or partner.
The specified benefit of P’s spouse or partner must be increased, starting on the date on which P’s main benefit is reduced, by an amount equal to the difference between—
the maximum rate of that specified benefit that is payable to a single person; and
the maximum rate of that specified benefit that is payable to a person who has a spouse or partner and whose spouse or partner is receiving a main benefit of the kind reduced.

See section 19 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001, under which a spouse’s or partner’s specified benefit is similarly increased when P is the beneficiary of New Zealand superannuation that is payable, as a result of P’s hospitalisation, at a reduced rate.
Nothing in this section entitles a single person or a couple who are in a relationship to be paid a greater amount of benefit, as a result of hospitalisation, than would otherwise have been payable.
In this section, specified benefit means—
a main benefit under this Act; or
New Zealand superannuation; or
a veteran’s pension.

history.history-note: Section 207(1)﻿(c): amended, on 9 November 2020, by section 40(1) of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).
Section 207(2): replaced, on 9 November 2020, by section 40(2) of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).
Section 207(2A): inserted, on 9 November 2020, by section 40(2) of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).
Section 207(4): inserted, on 9 November 2020, by section 40(3) of the New Zealand Superannuation and Veteran’s Pension Legislation Amendment Act 2020 (2020 No 36).

subpart.crosshead: Factors affecting benefit: alcohol and drug treatment

prov.heading: Beneficiary resident in institution for treatment of alcoholism or drug addiction

para.text: This section applies if a person (P) is—
a beneficiary; and
a resident of an institution for the treatment of alcoholism or drug addiction.
The rate of any benefit payable to P while a resident of the institution is determined by MSD after taking into account P’s personal financial circumstances.

In subsection (1), institution for the treatment of alcoholism or drug addiction means a treatment centre under the Substance Addiction (Compulsory Assessment and Treatment) Act 2017 (the 2017 Act) (not being a psychiatric security institution within the meaning of the Mental Health (Compulsory Assessment and Treatment) Act 1992) or any institution that fulfils a similar purpose to a treatment centre under the 2017 Act.

subpart.crosshead: Factors affecting benefit: issue of warrant for beneficiary’s arrest

prov.heading: Benefit not payable to person who is subject to warrant of arrest

para.text: A benefit is not payable to a person (P) if MSD is satisfied that—
P is a beneficiary who receives a main benefit under this Act, New Zealand superannuation, or a veteran’s pension; and
criminal proceedings have been commenced against P in a New Zealand court for an offence; and
a warrant has been issued by a New Zealand court (or judge or registrar of a New Zealand court) for P’s arrest in connection with those proceedings; and
the warrant has not been resolved and at least 28 days have elapsed since the date on which the warrant was issued.
However, MSD must not stop under this section payment of P’s benefit unless MSD has first—
followed the procedure set out in sections 211 and 212; and
considered P’s response, if any, to the unresolved warrant notice given under section 211.
A benefit the payment of which is stopped under this section remains not payable until MSD is satisfied that P is not the subject of the warrant or that the warrant has been resolved.
Nothing in this section affects the power of MSD to suspend immediately the benefit of a person under section 213.

prov.heading: Beneficiary and offence defined for section 209

para.text: In section 209,—
beneficiary excludes a person who is 1 of the following:
a young person (as defined in section 2(1) of the Oranga Tamariki Act 1989):
a person who falls within a class, description, or kind of beneficiary that is excluded from the operation of section 209 by regulations made under section 435
offence excludes an offence of a class, description, or kind excluded from the operation of section 209 by regulations made under section 435.

prov.heading: MSD must give beneficiary notice of unresolved warrant

para.text: As soon as practicable after MSD is notified of the issue of the warrant, MSD must give P a notice for the purposes of this section (an unresolved warrant notice) that complies with section 212.

prov.heading: Requirements for unresolved warrant notice

para.text: An unresolved warrant notice must—
specify a period of 10 working days within which the person to whom the notice is delivered (P) may—
dispute that P is the person to whom the warrant applies; or
take steps to resolve the warrant; and
specify the steps that P may take to dispute that P is the subject of the warrant or to resolve the warrant; and
specify the consequences if P fails to resolve the warrant within the 10-working-day period.

An unresolved warrant notice that involves information supplied by the Ministry of Justice under clause 15 of Schedule 6 may be combined with a notice of adverse action given under section 181(1) of the Privacy Act 2020.

history.history-note: Section 212(2): amended, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).

prov.heading: Immediate suspension of benefit at request of New Zealand Police

para.text: MSD may suspend immediately a benefit payable to a person (P) if—
MSD is satisfied that—
criminal proceedings have been commenced against P in a New Zealand court for an offence; and
a warrant has been issued in New Zealand for P’s arrest in connection with those proceedings; and
the warrant has not been resolved; and
the New Zealand Police has given MSD a written notice that—
states that the Commissioner of Police considers on reasonable grounds that P is a risk to public safety; and
requests MSD on the ground of that risk to suspend the benefit payable to P; and
is signed by the Commissioner or by a constable who occupies a position of the level of inspector or above.

prov.heading: Beneficiary and offence defined for section 213

para.text: In section 213,—
beneficiary excludes a person who is 1 of the following:
a young person (as defined in section 2(1) of the Oranga Tamariki Act 1989):
a person who falls within a class, description, or kind of beneficiary that is excluded from the operation of section 213 by regulations made under section 435
offence excludes an offence of a class, description, or kind excluded from the operation of section 213 by regulations made under section 435.

prov.heading: MSD must give beneficiary notice of immediate suspension

para.text: This section applies if under section 213 MSD suspends immediately a benefit payable to a person (P).
As soon as reasonably practicable, MSD must give P a written notice that—
states that the benefit has been suspended; and
states the reason for the suspension; and
specifies the steps that P may take to dispute that P is the subject of the warrant or to resolve the warrant.

prov.heading: Effect of non-payment or suspension of benefit

para.text: The following sections apply to a benefit (as if it had been suspended under section 232) that is not payable under section 209 or is suspended under section 213:
section 230:
section 245:
section 246:
section 248.

However, section 245 (which protects a spouse or partner) ceases to apply under subsection (1)﻿(b) to the benefit at the close of the 28th day after the date on which it becomes not payable or is suspended.
Subsection (1)﻿(b) does not apply if—
the person whose benefit is suspended under section 213 is not receiving a main benefit under this Act; or
the spouse or partner of the person whose benefit is suspended under section 213 is receiving a main benefit under this Act in the spouse’s or partner’s own right.

subpart.crosshead: Factors affecting benefit: custody in prison or on remand

prov.heading: Benefit not payable during custody in prison or on remand

para.text: A benefit is not payable to a person (P) for any period during which P is—
in custody in prison; or
in custody on remand; or
an escapee from custody in prison.
In this section and section 218,—
custody in prison means custody in prison under a sentence of imprisonment or preventive detention
custody on remand means custody on remand by a court pending a further appearance before a court.
For the purposes of this section,—
MSD may regard as time spent in custody in prison or custody on remand time spent by a beneficiary in a hospital during a period of custody; and
a person does not cease to be in custody by being released for a period of 24 hours or less for a purpose authorised by the Corrections Act 2004.

prov.heading: Exceptions to rule that benefit not payable during custody in prison or on remand

para.text: MSD may continue payment to a beneficiary of New Zealand superannuation or a veteran’s pension for 2 pay days after the first day of custody in prison.
MSD may pay all or part of a benefit to—
the spouse or partner of a person in custody in prison or of an escapee from that custody; or
any responsible person for the benefit of—
that spouse or partner; or
the dependent child or children of a person in custody in prison or of an escapee from that custody.
MSD may pay all or part of a benefit to a beneficiary in custody on remand after taking into account the beneficiary’s personal financial circumstances.

subpart.crosshead: Factors affecting benefit: absence from New Zealand

prov.heading: General rule: benefit not payable while beneficiary absent from New Zealand

para.text: For any period during which a person is absent from New Zealand, a benefit is not payable to that person.
However, this section does not apply to a benefit payable under—
subpart 11 of Part 2 (winter energy payment); or
sections 21 to 35 of the New Zealand Superannuation and Retirement Income Act 2001; or
sections 176 to 196 of the Veterans' Support Act 2014; or
any reciprocity agreement with another country adopted by an order made under section 380.
This section is also subject to the exceptions, or other contrary or related provisions, contained in regulations made under section 436.

prov.heading: Special absence rule: winter energy payment

para.text: The winter energy payment is not payable for 1 or more days while a beneficiary is absent from New Zealand unless it is so payable under subsection (2).

The winter energy payment is payable to a beneficiary for up to a maximum of 28 days of any 1 or more absences (however long each absence lasts in total) of the beneficiary from New Zealand during the winter period (as defined in section 71) if—
the payment would be payable to the beneficiary were it not for those days of those absences; and
MSD is satisfied that those days of those absences do not affect the beneficiary’s eligibility for the payment under section 72.
Sections 219 and 436 do not apply to the winter energy payment.

history.history-note: Section 220(2): replaced, on 1 May 2019, by section 5 of the Social Security (Winter Energy Payment) Amendment Act 2019 (2019 No 13).

subpart.crosshead: Factors affecting benefit: additional dependent child

history.history-note: Heading: repealed, on 8 November 2021, by section 7 of the Social Security (Subsequent Child Policy Removal) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 38).

prov.heading: Purpose of sections 222 to 224

history.history-note: Section 221: repealed, on 8 November 2021, by section 7 of the Social Security (Subsequent Child Policy Removal) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 38).

prov.heading: Additional dependent child must not be taken into account in certain cases

history.history-note: Section 222: repealed, on 8 November 2021, by section 7 of the Social Security (Subsequent Child Policy Removal) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 38).

prov.heading: Extended application of additional dependent child rules

history.history-note: Section 223: repealed, on 8 November 2021, by section 7 of the Social Security (Subsequent Child Policy Removal) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 38).

prov.heading: Limited application of additional dependent child rules

history.history-note: Section 224: repealed, on 8 November 2021, by section 7 of the Social Security (Subsequent Child Policy Removal) Amendment Act 2021 (2021 No 38).

subpart.crosshead: Factors affecting benefit: voluntary unemployment or loss of employment through misconduct, etc

prov.heading: Basic rule: no work-tested benefit for 13 weeks after leaving employment or scheme or loss of employment due to misconduct

para.text: A person is not entitled to a work-tested benefit for a period of 13 weeks after ceasing employment if MSD is satisfied that the person has—
left that employment voluntarily without good and sufficient reason; or
lost that employment because of misconduct as an employee.
A person is not entitled to a work-tested benefit for a period of 13 weeks after ceasing to participate in a scheme if MSD is satisfied that the person—
has been receiving payments under the scheme and has voluntarily ceased to participate in the scheme without good and sufficient reason; or
has ceased to participate in the scheme because of misconduct.
This section is subject to section 323.

prov.heading: Persons to whom basic rule applies

para.text: The basic rule applies to the following persons:
a work-tested beneficiary:
an applicant for a benefit who would become a work-tested beneficiary if the benefit were granted:
the spouse or partner of an applicant for a couple rate of benefit who would become a work-tested spouse or partner if the benefit were granted:
an applicant for jobseeker support on the ground of health condition, injury, or disability who has the capacity for part-time work.

prov.heading: What happens if basic rule applies

para.text: If the basic rule applies to a person (P),—
MSD must cancel the benefit (if granted); and
must not grant P the benefit in any other case; and
P is not entitled, during the 13-week period, to the cancelled benefit or a work-tested benefit.
However, under section 228, MSD may decide not to apply subsection (1).
Subsection (1) is subject to sections 246 to 248, 320, 323, and 324.

prov.heading: MSD’s options in case of misconduct

para.text: If the basic rule applies to a person (P) because of misconduct, MSD has 2 additional options:
MSD may decide that the basic rule should not apply to P; or

MSD may decide that the benefit should be paid to P, or paid without reduction, subject to the condition that MSD may require P to repay the benefit or part of it if a court or person or body authorised by law to determine the matter determines the misconduct to be proved.

Any amount that MSD requires P to repay under subsection (1)﻿(b) is a debt due to the Crown under regulations made under section 444 and may be recovered from P under regulations made under section 444.

prov.heading: Interpretation

para.text: For the purposes of this section and sections 225 to 228,—
basic rule means the rule set out in section 225
benefit means a work-tested benefit
employment,—
in the case of an applicant for a work-tested benefit, means—
full-time employment; or

part-time employment if MSD is satisfied that, for a period of not less than 13 weeks preceding the termination of the employment, the income from that employment was sufficient to maintain the person; and
in the case of a part-time work-tested beneficiary, means regular part-time work; and
in the case of any other work-tested beneficiary, means full-time employment; but
does not include redundancy
scheme means a Government-assisted scheme that MSD considers analogous to a benefit.

subpart.crosshead: Factors affecting benefits: non-entitlement period, stand down, or 100% suspension of benefit

prov.heading: How non-entitlement period, etc, affects supplementary benefits, and spouse or partner

para.text: This section applies while a person is subject to—
a non-entitlement period; or
a stand down under section 316 (start and calculation of stand-down period); or
a reduction of the rate of a main benefit to zero under section 236, 236A, 236C, 236F, or 236I; or
a sanction of suspension of 100% of a benefit under section 237, 270, or 280.
The person is not entitled to receive—
an emergency benefit; or
an accommodation supplement; or
temporary additional support.

However, an accommodation supplement that an applicant was receiving immediately before the applicant applied for a benefit continues, despite subsections (1)﻿(b) and (2)﻿(b), at the same rate for the period of non-entitlement.

If a person’s spouse or partner is under section 316 (start and calculation of stand-down period) not entitled to a benefit, then (under this subsection, and despite any contrary provision of this Act) the person is also not entitled to the benefit.

history.history-note: Section 230(1)﻿(ba): inserted, on 26 May 2025, by section 12(1) of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).
Section 230(1)﻿(ba): amended, on 20 October 2025, by section 12(2) of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).

part.heading: Enforcement: sanctions and offences

prov.heading: What this Part does

para.text: This Part sets out—
the sanctions that may be imposed for a breach of an obligation under this Act:
the offences that are created by this Act.

subpart.heading: Sanctions for breach of obligations other than young person or young parent obligations

prov.heading: Sanctions for failure to comply with certain obligations under this Act

para.text: MSD must under this section impose a sanction if MSD considers that a person has failed without good and sufficient reason to comply with 1 or more obligations under this Act that are specified in section 233.
The sanction is 1 of the following (in accordance with section 234):
reduction of the rate of a main benefit or imposition of a non-financial sanction in accordance with sections 236, 236A, 236B, 236E, and 236H:
suspension of a main benefit:
cancellation of a main benefit.
MSD must not impose under this section a non-financial sanction on a young person to whom section 276(2)﻿(a) or (b) or 278(2)﻿(a) or (b) applies.
This section is subject to sections 243AAA, 243, and 245 to 248.

history.history-note: Section 232(2)﻿(a): replaced, on 26 May 2025, by section 13(1) of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).
Section 232(2)﻿(a): amended, on 20 October 2025, by section 13(2) of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).
Section 232(3): inserted, on 26 May 2025, by section 13(3) of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).
Section 232(4): inserted, on 26 May 2025, by section 13(3) of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).

prov.heading: Obligations that carry sanction for failure to comply

para.text: The obligations referred to in section 232(1) are the following:
obligation to comply with a requirement under section 118 to attend and participate in a work ability assessment or reassessment:
obligation to comply with a work-preparation obligation under section 124 or 125:
obligation to comply with an obligation under sections 131 to 135 in relation to dependent children:
obligation to comply with a work-test obligation under section 144 or 146:
A work-test obligation—
that, under section 147(2), includes the obligation under section 147(1) to undertake and pass a drug test.
obligation to comply with a requirement under section 158(4) to attend an interview:
obligation to comply with a requirement under section 170(2) in relation to working with contracted service providers.

prov.label: 233A
prov.heading: Cancellation of main benefit for continuing failure to comply

para.text: MSD must cancel a person’s (P’s) main benefit if—
MSD has, under section 232, imposed a sanction described in subsection (2)﻿(a) or (b) of that section on P; and
P fails to recomply, within 13 weeks after the sanction takes effect, with each obligation—
for which the sanction was imposed for a failure to comply with; and
that P has not ceased to have (including, without limitation, under section 321 or 322); and
section 320 does not apply to P.
On cancellation under this section of P’s main benefit, to be again entitled to a main benefit under this Act, P must apply for the benefit and establish that P is eligible for it.
This section is subject to sections 236D(3)﻿(d), 236G(3)﻿(d), 236J(3)﻿(d), and 245 to 248.

history.history-note: Section 233A: inserted, on 26 May 2025, by section 14(1) of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).
Section 233A(3): amended, on 20 October 2025, by section 14(2) of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).

prov.heading: Hierarchy of sanctions

para.text: The sanction that MSD must impose under section 232(1) (subject to section 232(3) and (4)) is,—
for a first failure, reduction of the rate of a main benefit or imposition of a non-financial sanction in accordance with sections 236, 236A, 236B, 236E, and 236H:
for a second failure, suspension of a main benefit:
for a third failure, cancellation of a main benefit.

history.history-note: Section 234: amended, on 26 May 2025, by section 15(1) of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).
Section 234(a): replaced, on 26 May 2025, by section 15(2) of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).
Section 234(a): amended, on 20 October 2025, by section 15(3) of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).

prov.heading: Failure, and first, second, and third failure, defined for obligations other than young person or young parent obligations

para.text: In this subpart,—
failure, in relation to a person, means a failure—
by the person, or in relation to the person’s dependent children or the person’s spouse or partner’s dependent children, to comply with an obligation that is specified in section 233; and

that occurred at any time (however, if it occurred more than 24 months or, if applicable, 12 months, before the failure for which the calculation is made, then see also section 242(1) and (3) and, if applicable, the express modifications made to section 242(1) and (3) by sections 287 and 287A); and

that, for the purposes of section 242(1), is taken to have occurred on the date MSD decides that the person has, as a beneficiary, failed, without a good and sufficient reason, to comply with the appropriate obligation (see section 242(2))
first failure, in relation to a person, means a failure (as defined in this section)—
by the person, or in relation to the person’s dependent children or the person’s spouse or partner’s dependent children; and
that is the first one by the person, or in relation to the person’s dependent children or the person’s spouse or partner’s dependent children
second failure, in relation to a person, means a failure (as defined in this section)—
by the person, or in relation to the person’s dependent children or the person’s spouse or partner’s dependent children; and
that occurs after the person has recomplied with the obligation to which a first failure related
third failure, in relation to a person, means a failure (as defined in this section)—
by the person, or in relation to the person’s dependent children or the person’s spouse or partner’s dependent children; and
that occurs after the person has recomplied with the obligation to which a second failure related.

history.history-note: Section 235 failure: replaced, on 26 May 2025, by section 16 of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).
Section 235 first failure: replaced, on 26 May 2025, by section 16 of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).
Section 235 second failure: replaced, on 26 May 2025, by section 16 of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).
Section 235 third failure: replaced, on 26 May 2025, by section 16 of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).

prov.heading: Sanction for first failure: reduction of rate of main benefit

para.text: This section applies to a person (P) if P has a first failure, and either—
P is ineligible for a non-financial sanction because P does not meet the eligibility requirements in any of the following provisions:
section 236A(1)﻿(b):
section 236B(1)﻿(b) to (e):
section 236E(1)﻿(b) to (e):
section 236H(1)﻿(b) to (d); or

P is eligible for a non-financial sanction because P meets the eligibility requirements in all or any of the provisions specified in paragraph (a)﻿(i) to (iv), and MSD does not impose a non-financial sanction for P’s first failure.
If this section applies to P, MSD must, for the first failure of P, reduce the rate of P’s main benefit by half until P recomplies.
If the rate of P’s main benefit is reduced by half under this section, MSD must not, for the first failure of P, impose a non-financial sanction.
If P fails to recomply within 4 weeks after the initial reduction under this section takes effect, MSD must reduce the rate of P’s main benefit to zero until the earlier of the following events:
P recomplies:
MSD cancels P’s main benefit under section 233A.
This section is subject to sections 243AAA, 243, 245 to 248, and 320 to 322.

history.history-note: Section 236: replaced, on 26 May 2025, by section 17 of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).
Section 236(1)﻿(a): replaced, on 20 October 2025, by section 18(1) of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).
Section 236(1)﻿(b): replaced, on 20 October 2025, by section 18(1) of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).
Section 236(3): replaced, on 20 October 2025, by section 18(2) of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).

prov.label: 236A
prov.heading: Sanction for first failure: money management

para.text: This section applies to a person (P) if—
P has a first failure; and
either—
P is subject to active case management by MSD; or
P has 1 or more dependent children; and
MSD is satisfied—
that it is appropriate in the circumstances to impose money management on P; and
that it is more appropriate to impose that sanction than any other sanction that can be imposed for P’s first failure.
If this section applies to P, MSD must, for the first failure of P, impose money management—
under section 232(2)﻿(a) and this section; and
only if, in the 5 working days from the giving of the section 252 notice, P discusses with MSD, in an appointment with MSD, P’s failure to comply.
If money management is imposed on P, MSD must not, for the first failure of P,—
reduce the rate of P’s main benefit by half under section 236(2); or
impose a non-financial sanction other than money management.
While money management that is imposed on P has effect,—

the prescribed proportion of an instalment of P’s main benefit that is an instalment payable to P (see regulations made under section 418(1)﻿(ia)) is paid by the required manner of payment for money management (see regulations made under section 418(1)﻿(jb)):
the rest of the instalment is paid under section 339:
specified arrears are credited or paid, and specified debt is recovered, under regulations made under section 418(1)﻿(jb).

If P fails to recomply (see section 261A) within the prescribed period starting when the sanction takes effect under subsection (6)﻿(a) (see regulations made under section 418(1)﻿(ib)), MSD must reduce the rate of P’s main benefit to zero until the earlier of the following events:
P recomplies (see section 261A):
MSD cancels P’s main benefit under section 233A.
Money management that is imposed on P has effect for the following duration:
from the date stated in the notice of non-financial sanction under section 252A(2)﻿(c); and

until the end of the prescribed period from that date (see regulations made under section 418(1)﻿(ib)), even if P recomplies (that is, both of the things specified in section 261A(2)﻿(a) and (b) have occurred) within the prescribed period.
Subsection (6)﻿(b) applies even if P’s main benefit is suspended for reasons unrelated to a sanction for failure to comply with 1 or more obligations under this Act that are specified in section 233.
This section is subject to sections 243AAA, 243, 245 to 248, and 320 to 322.

history.history-note: Section 236A: inserted, on 26 May 2025, by section 17 of the Social Security Amendment Act 2025 (2025 No 25).

prov.label: 236B
prov.heading: Sanction for first failure: community work experience: general

para.text: This section applies to a person (P) if—
P has a first failure; and
either—
P is subject to active case management by MSD; or
P has 1 or more dependent children; and
P’s first failure is not a failure to comply with a drug-testing obligation; and
P has—
work-test obligations under either or both of sections 144 and 146; and
no currently-in-force deferrals of, or exemptions from, those work-test obligations; and
P’s first failure does not involve either or both of the following:
more than 1 failure of the same obligation:
a failure of more than 1 obligation; and
MSD is satisfied—
that it is appropriate in the circumstances to impose community work experience on P; and
that it is more appropriate to impose that sanction than any other sanction that can be imposed for P’s first failure.
If this section applies to P, MSD must, for the first failure of P, impose community work experience—
under section 232(2)﻿(a) and this section; and
only if, in the 5 working days from the giving of the section 252 notice, P discusses with MSD, in an appointment with MSD, P’s failure to comply.
If community work experience is imposed on P, MSD must not, for the first failure of P, do the following (except under section 236D):
reduce the rate of P’s main benefit by half under section 236(2); or
impose a non-financial sanction other than community work experience.
While community work experience that is imposed on P has effect,—

P must, within the prescribed search period starting when the sanction takes effect under subsection (5)﻿(a) (see regulations made under section 418(1)﻿(ic)), and with the support from MSD (if any) that MSD considers it appropriate to provide to P for the purpose, find 1 or more positions—
each of which is a position—
with a community or voluntary sector organisation that MSD is satisfied is reasonably suitable; and
that MSD is satisfied is reasonably suitable; and
that alone is, or that together are, for at least the following (see regulations made under section 418(1)﻿(id)):
the prescribed minimum number of hours per week; and
the prescribed minimum number of weeks that start as specified in paragraph (b)﻿(ii); and
